General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

How Much Does Pilot Training Cost In The US?

The cost of pilot training extends beyond tuition fees, living expenses, simulator time, and other charges that can catch aspiring pilots off guard.

Hidden costs like housing, simulator fees, and checkride expenses can nearly double the stated tuition price, creating significant financial barriers for career-seeking pilots.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Why The C-5M Super Galaxy's 28 Wheels Must Rotate 90 Degrees Just To Retract

The brilliant engineering solution enables a massive payload and efficient flight performance.

The C-5M's 90-degree wheel rotation mechanism solves a fundamental constraint between fuselage design and landing gear geometry, demonstrating how payload capacity directly influences structural innovation in ultra-large aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran Attained A Fleet Of F-14s Even Though The US Regretted It – Now They’re Destroyed

During the Cold War, Iran was viewed as a crucial stop-block for Soviet southern expansion, the F-14 played a role in this effort.

Iran's F-14 fleet, acquired during the Cold War as a geopolitical counter to Soviet expansion, represents a historic example of how military aircraft exports can have unintended long-term consequences for global stability.

See Simple Flying →

We are looking for a practical, capable Development Engineer to join our team in a hands-on engineering...

Light Dynamix is actively recruiting specialized drone engineers, signaling expansion in commercial UAV development capabilities.

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

The 7 Longest Boeing 787 Dreamliner Routes In The World

Discover the seven longest Boeing 787 flights of 2026 and the airlines that make them possible.

Ultra-long-range 787 operations are enabling airlines to establish direct point-to-point routes that bypass traditional hub systems, reducing passenger travel time and improving network economics.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective SRM requires organizations to systematically document risk control rationale and demonstrate why residual risks are acceptable to stakeholders, not just identify hazards.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

Why Hawaiian Airlines Pilots Must Shave Their Beards During Alaska Integration

Hawaiian pilots will no longer be allowed beards under new Alaska uniform standards.

Alaska's beard policy enforcement during the merger signals broader operational standardization that could affect pilot recruitment and retention in a competitive labor market.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Artemis 2 in good shape cruising towards the moon

A day after lighting its engine to head to the moon, the Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft is performing well with only minor issues.

Artemis 2's successful trans-lunar injection burn demonstrates NASA's readiness to resume crewed lunar missions after years of delays and technical refinements.

See SpaceNews →

Regulatory Apr 4, 2026

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

Cutting EAS funding threatens connectivity to rural markets, potentially forcing regional carriers to abandon unprofitable routes and concentrate service in hub-dominated networks.

See AeroTime Hub →

PAI
Safety Apr 4, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

A robust reporting culture requires psychological safety and non-punitive responses to errors, which directly correlates with SMS effectiveness and accident prevention rates.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Military Apr 4, 2026

What It’s Like Flying In A Military Transport Aircraft

Uncle Sam's no-frills flying experience.

Military transport prioritizes cargo and personnel efficiency over passenger comfort, making civilian charter operations appear luxurious by comparison.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

What United Airlines' New Relax Row Economy Beds Will Likely Cost On Long-Haul Flights

These beds could come with an appealing price tag.

United's economy bed pricing strategy may undercut premium cabin revenue by offering lie-flat comfort at economy fares, fundamentally reshaping long-haul cabin economics.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's upgraded Cygnus XL variant demonstrates increased cargo capacity at 11,000 pounds, expanding commercial resupply economics for ISS operations.

See NASA →

▶
General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Eastern Iowa Airport's diverse traffic mix from trainers to air carriers creates a unique training environment where flight schools benefit from exposure to varied aircraft operations and commercial airport procedures.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

6 Airlines With The World’s Longest Economy Seat Pitch

Which airlines are currently offering the most seat pitch for their respective economy class products? Here are 6 airlines that have mastered space.

Airlines prioritizing 32+ inch economy pitch are capturing premium leisure segments without raising base fares, reshaping competition beyond traditional premium cabins.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Why Do Airlines Still Use 3-3 Seating On Narrowbody Jets?

Narrowbody aircraft are limited by regulations to max out at 3-3 seating, while changing their fundamental design means they are not narrowbodies.

Airlines cannot increase narrowbody density beyond 3-3 seating without regulatory recertification that would reclassify aircraft and require costly redesigns.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US A-10 hit during Iran rescue mission, pilot ejects and is rescued

A US Air Force A-10 was hit during combat operations tied to the widening war with Iran on…...

Combat damage to A-10s during extended Middle East operations highlights vulnerabilities in aging airframes during high-threat missions requiring sustained air support.

See AeroTime Hub →

▶
General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Frederick Municipal Airport, MD (FDK)

Frederick Municipal is a small general and corporate aviation airport located just east of the city of Frederick, Maryland. The traffic mix runs the spectrum from single engine training aircraft up...

Frederick Municipal Airport serves as a critical training hub and corporate gateway for mid-Atlantic aviation, balancing flight instruction operations with business travel demand in a resource-constrained regional facility.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Military Apr 3, 2026

The World’s Largest Air Forces By Annual Budget

A closer look at the world leaders in air power spending.

US air force spending exceeds the combined budgets of the next ten countries, revealing a significant global disparity in military aviation capability and modernization capacity.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's pilot background positions the NTSB to strengthen technical credibility in investigating aviation accidents and influencing safety policy.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

US and Europe revise GPS interference guidelines as spoofing risks grow

Aviation authorities are warning that instances of GPS interference have spread across a widening range of high-risk airspace,…...

Expanding GPS spoofing incidents across critical airspace are forcing regulators to update operational standards, signaling aviation's growing vulnerability to intentional signal manipulation.

See AeroTime Hub →

Industry Apr 4, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-airspace systems offers greater near-term emissions and capacity gains than waiting for breakthrough aircraft technologies.

See MDPI →

Military Apr 3, 2026

A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashed Near the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Officials Tell NYT

Almost the same time as an F-15E Strike Eagle went down over Iran, the U.S. Air Force lost an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft near the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. officials told NYT that the jet’s pilot was safely rescued. While search and rescue efforts for at least one of the crew members of an […]...

The simultaneous loss of two U.S. Air Force jets in the Middle East region suggests sustained operational tempo or heightened threat environment requiring immediate incident investigation.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Meet NASA’s New Artemis II Science Officers

NASA’s first Artemis II science officers—Kelsey Young, Trevor Graff, and Angela Garcia—are pioneering a new era of mission operations by integrating advanced lunar science directly into Mission Control. Their work will shape how future Artemis crews observe, analyze, and deepen our understanding of...

Embedding specialized science officers directly in Mission Control represents a structural shift in how NASA operationalizes lunar research, enabling real-time scientific decision-making rather than post-mission analysis.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Italy’s Argotec plans to scale Florida satellite facility to meet rising US demand

Italy’s Argotec has officially opened its first U.S. satellite production facility, cementing a foothold near Kennedy Space Center in Florida to join other foreign space firms pursuing growing demand from American programs.

Argotec's Florida facility positions European satellite manufacturers to capture increased U.S. government and commercial demand while bypassing supply chain vulnerabilities.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US F-15E Shot Down Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued

The fate of the aircraft’s second crew member is unknown.

Combat loss of a US fighter jet over Iran demonstrates persistent air defense threats in the region and raises operational risks for future missions.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA report proposes sweeping rewrite of Part 141 flight training rules

A 471-page industry report released through the FAA’s Part 141 modernization docket proposes a sweeping rewrite of how…...

The proposed 471-page rewrite aims to modernize outdated Part 141 standards that haven't substantially changed in decades, potentially streamlining training curricula and approval processes for flight schools.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom

Today marks the 100th anniversary of the birth of Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom, born April 3, 1926, in Mitchell, Indiana. As one of NASA’s first seven astronauts, he became America’s second astronaut to fly in space when he launched aboard the Liberty Bell 7 spacecraft on July 21, 1961, just weeks after...

Gus Grissom's pioneering Mercury program flights established critical protocols for human spaceflight that remain foundational to modern aerospace operations.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 3, 2026

A C-130J awaits its next assignment on the flightline.

The C-130J's continued presence on active flightlines demonstrates sustained operational readiness for military airlift missions decades after the platform's introduction.

See AVweb →

Electric Apr 3, 2026

ZeroAvia edges closer to cert as FAA issues electric engine conditions

ZeroAvia has this week celebrated a “major step” towards certification of its ZA600 hydrogen-electric powertrain, after the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) published special conditions to certify the system’s electric engine component. The FAA published 33 special conditions for...

FAA's 33 special conditions establish the first regulatory framework for hydrogen-electric propulsion, setting precedent for future alternative-energy aircraft certifications.

See Runway Girl Network →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

White House again proposes steep NASA budget cuts

For the second consecutive year, the White House is proposing a major budget cut for NASA that would significantly impact the agency’s science programs and the International Space Station.

Sustained NASA budget reductions threaten ISS operations and Earth science capabilities that support aviation weather forecasting and climate modeling critical for flight planning.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Test Pilots and the Road Back to the Moon

For NASA’s first crewed lunar mission since Apollo, aviation experience is one of the clearest threads linking the astronauts aboard Orion.

NASA's Artemis lunar program prioritizes selecting test pilots with extensive high-performance aircraft experience to manage Orion's unprecedented re-entry dynamics and operational unknowns.

See AVweb →

Drones Apr 3, 2026

New DJI Terra update adds HEIF support, boosts reconstruction performance

DJI is continuing to refine its mapping and modeling ecosystem with the latest update to DJI Terra, and version 5.2.0 brings a mix of speed boosts, smarter automation, and improved output quality for enterprise users. more…...

HEIF support and reconstruction performance improvements position DJI Terra to compete more effectively in enterprise mapping workflows where file efficiency and processing speed directly impact project delivery costs.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Hello, World

NASA astronaut and Artemis II Commander Reid Wiseman took this picture of Earth from the Orion spacecraft’s window after completing the translunar injection burn. There are two auroras (top right and bottom left) and zodiacal light (bottom right) is visible as the Earth eclipses the Sun. This and...

Artemis II's translunar trajectory now enables unprecedented Earth observation capabilities that could inform climate monitoring and atmospheric research from lunar distances.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US rescues one airman after F-15E shot down over Iran, search continues for second

Unverified images circulating on social media on April 3, 2026, appear to show the wreckage of a US…...

Combat loss over Iran underscores escalating air defense threats to US military operations in contested airspace where pilot survival and recovery operations face significant geopolitical complexity.

See AeroTime Hub →

Drones Apr 3, 2026

US drone future hinges on policy, investment, coordination

The Commercial Drone Alliance is pushing Washington to move faster, and more strategically, on building a truly domestic drone ecosystem. In a newly released white paper, the industry group calls on the Trump Administration to adopt sweeping reforms aimed at strengthening US manufacturing, creating...

The Commercial Drone Alliance's strategic push for federal coordination reveals that regulatory clarity and domestic manufacturing investment are now critical competitive factors against international drone market dominance.
